content.problemStatement:
Consider any way to pair the integers 111 through 2N2N2N into NNN pairs (A1,B1),(A2,B2),‚Ä¶,(AN,BN)(A_1,B_1), (A_2,B_2), \ldots, (A_N,B_N)(A1‚Äã,B1‚Äã),(A2‚Äã,B2‚Äã),‚Ä¶,(AN‚Äã,BN‚Äã) in such a way that each of these integers is present in exactly one pair, Ai<BiA_i \lt B_iAi‚Äã<Bi‚Äã for each valid iii and A1<A2<‚Ä¶<ANA_1 \lt A_2 \lt \ldots \lt A_NA1‚Äã<A2‚Äã<‚Ä¶<AN‚Äã. Let's call such a sequence of pairs a pairing.

For a pairing (A1,B1),(A2,B2),‚Ä¶,(AN,BN)(A_1,B_1), (A_2,B_2), \ldots, (A_N,B_N)(A1‚Äã,B1‚Äã),(A2‚Äã,B2‚Äã),‚Ä¶,(AN‚Äã,BN‚Äã), we can create an undirected graph with 2N2N2N nodes (numbered 111 through 2N2N2N) and the following edges:


first type: for each valid iii, an edge between nodes iii and i+1i+1i+1
second type: for each valid iii, an edge between nodes AiA_iAi‚Äã and BiB_iBi‚Äã
This pairing is valid if for each edge (i,i+1)(i,i+1)(i,i+1) of the first type, there is an edge of the second type (Aj,Bj)(A_j, B_j)(Aj‚Äã,Bj‚Äã) which "surrounds" it, i.e. satisfies Aj‚â§iA_j \le iAj‚Äã‚â§i and Bj>iB_j \gt iBj‚Äã>i. The cost of a valid pairing is defined as the number of pairs of edges of the second type such that one of these edges is "nested" in the other, i.e. pairs (Ai,Bi)(A_i, B_i)(Ai‚Äã,Bi‚Äã) and (Aj,Bj)(A_j, B_j)(Aj‚Äã,Bj‚Äã) such that Ai<Aj<Bj<BiA_i \lt A_j \lt B_j \lt B_iAi‚Äã<Aj‚Äã<Bj‚Äã<Bi‚Äã.

Find the sum of costs of all valid pairings. Since this number may be very large, compute it modulo a given prime PPP.
constraints: the sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 10,00010,00010,000
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first and only line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and PPP.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer ‚Äî the sum of costs of all valid pairings modulo PPP.
